---
date: "2026-07-27"
---

## What's Clever in Each Paper

- Part 5 — the one genuinely good idea in each paper

::: notes
Welcome back to Chapter six-point-four, part five of seven. In part four we read both papers table by table and watched the same shape emerge twice: a large raw gap, a decomposition that explains much of it with legitimate risk, and a residual that survives benchmarking to the machine. Now we name the one genuinely good idea in each paper — because naming cleverness is how Maya learns to be clever herself. In part six we will push back like a referee; here we give credit where it is due.
:::

## What's Clever: Bartlett et al.

- Use a **formula-priced market** to manufacture a clean risk benchmark
- "Did they price legitimate risk?" becomes "did they price **more** than the formula?"
- Then **technology as a natural split**: vary only the human in the loop
- Same instinct as a clean comparison in Week 4

::: notes
Bartlett et al.'s cleverness is using a formula-priced market to manufacture a clean legitimate-risk benchmark, and then using technology as a natural split. Because government-sponsored-enterprise loans are priced off a published risk formula, the authors do not have to argue about what the fair price is — the institution tells them. That converts the impossible question "did they price legitimate risk?" into the answerable question "did they price more than the formula's risk?" Then the FinTech-versus-human split turns the abstract worry "is the algorithm the cure?" into a measurable contrast: hold the question fixed and vary only whether a human was in the loop. It is the same instinct as a clean comparison in Week Four — find a setting where the thing you want to hold fixed is fixed by the world.
:::

## What's Clever: BHR

- Getting the score **measures** the OVB the literature could only fear
- The race-blind AUS is a **found counterfactual**, not a lab construction
- The "same applicant, vary only the protected trait" of Chapter 3.1
- Shared cleverness: separate **pricing** from **acceptance** with millions of loans

::: notes
B-H-R's cleverness is twofold. First, simply observing the credit score is a contribution, because it lets them measure the omitted-variable bias the whole disclosure-only literature could only fear — turning Mentor Four's "you can never see the score" into "here is what happens to the coefficient when you finally can." Second, the race-blind automated underwriting system is a found experiment: a real, deployed decision rule that by design cannot use race, serving as the "same-quality applicant, vary only the protected trait" counterfactual that Chapter three-point-one says you can never directly observe. They did not have to build the counterfactual in a lab — the mortgage system built one for them. The deepest shared cleverness across both papers is separating pricing discrimination from acceptance discrimination using big loan-level data. Audit studies, like Mentor Four's Bertrand–Mullainathan résumés, randomize the trait but observe only a callback. These papers cannot randomize, but they observe millions of real loans on both margins, so they can ask where in the process the disparity lives.
:::

## Key Takeaway

::: callout-tip
## Key takeaway
Clever: Bartlett et al. use a **formula-priced benchmark** plus a technology split; BHR **measure the OVB** and exploit a **found race-blind counterfactual**. Shared masterstroke: separating pricing from acceptance with millions of loans. Next: where each is **vulnerable**.
:::

::: notes
To summarize part five: each paper has one genuinely good idea. Bartlett et al. use a formula-priced market as a clean legitimate-risk benchmark, then add a technology split that varies only whether a human is in the loop. B-H-R measure the omitted-variable bias the literature could only fear, and turn a deployed race-blind engine into a found counterfactual. And the shared masterstroke is separating pricing discrimination from acceptance discrimination using millions of real loans on both margins. In part six we turn the question around and push back the way a hostile referee will — finding the soft spots Mentor Four promised would be attacked first.
:::
